exemplar.title: Determining Faster Detection of a Target Letter Among Round and Angular Letter Lists

detailedExemplarData.title: Determining Faster Detection of a Target Letter Among Round and Angular Letter Lists

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, precise aim with strong rationale linking Perceptual Cycle Model to real-world tasks
Accurate description of theory/model and explicit connection to the letter-search task
Well-stated null and research hypotheses with fully operationalized IV and DV
weaknesses: Real-world relevance is concise and could be expanded with more primary literature citations
Hypotheses could specify timing procedures (e.g., stopwatch start/stop) more precisely
Use of nonâpeer-reviewed sources reduces theoretical rigor
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Introduction demonstrates a thorough understanding of background theory and practical relevance, fulfilling the top band descriptors. The aim is precisely stated and well motivated, and the Perceptual Cycle Model is accurately described with a clear link to the investigation. Hypotheses are fully operationalized. Minor brevity in real-world examples and limited academic sourcing constrain this section to a solid 5 rather than the maximum.

strengths: Research design is not only described but clearly explained, with justification for choosing a repeated-measures approach
Sampling technique is detailed and linked to study constraints (excluding psychology students)
Controlled variables are listed with brief justifications for room conditions and participant instructions
weaknesses: Key term âinvalidâ misused when discussing individual differencesâterminology should be corrected
No discussion of sample representativeness or demographic effects on external validity
Stopwatch calibration and timing reliability are not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Exploration section meets the upper descriptor for design and sampling by providing explanations rather than mere descriptions. However, the discussion of controlled variables is cursory and omits critical equipment considerations, and sampling rationale lacks depth on representativeness. These limitations place the work in the lower half of the top band (3/4).

strengths: Descriptive and inferential statistics (paired t-test) are appropriately applied with correct values for df, t, p and confidence interval
Statistical findings are clearly interpreted and linked back to the hypothesis, demonstrating strong analytical insight
Descriptive statistics table is well organized, facilitating comparison between conditions
weaknesses: Graphs lack error bars and clear axis units, impairing visual interpretation
Final report omits t-statistic and CI in the concluding sentence
Effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) is not computed or reported, and units are inconsistently applied
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Analysis section applies inferential statistics correctly and interprets results in line with the hypothesis. However, graphical presentation errors and omissions in reporting key statistics (effect size, consistent units) weaken the overall performance, justifying a mid-top-band score of 4.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to Neisserâs work and the Perceptual Cycle Model, situating results within theory
Strengths and limitations are identified and directly linked to study validity and reliability
Modifications are explicitly tied to limitations and justified (e.g., varied testing times, broader age range)
weaknesses: Evaluation could be deepened by considering alternative cognitive theories (e.g., feature integration theory)
Discussion omits potential bias from gender imbalance in the sample
Suggestions lack mention of counterbalancing to address order effects and do not report effect size or practical implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Evaluation is coherent and aligns findings with background theory, and the proposed modifications directly address identified limitations. Some omissionsâalternative theoretical perspectives, sample bias, and additional procedural controlsâlimit the depth of critique, placing this section at 4 rather than the top mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two graphs directly address the hypothesis (mean comparison bar and individual paired bars).  However, axis labels are minimal (units missing on one graph) and error bars are absent, reflecting **presentation inaccuracies**.  This matches the rubric description of a graph that supports the hypothesis but contains errors.

The student clearly states that the null hypothesis is rejected (p < 0.0008) and interprets the finding in terms of perceptual analysis and distractor effects, explicitly linking results to the hypothesis.  Interpretation is correct and data-based, though not deeply statistical; hence a secure 5 in the top band.

Both null and research hypotheses are given.  The IV (type of distractor list: round vs angular letters) and the DV (time in seconds to locate âZâ) are **clearly named and fully operationalised** (50-letter displays, stopwatch timing).  No ambiguities remain, so the strand merits the top of the highest band.

Convenience sampling is named, the procedure for accessing participants is detailed, and **reasons are provided** (accessibility, excluding students with prior psychology knowledge).  The choice is explicitly connected to the investigationâs needs, so an explanatory, not merely descriptive, level is reached.

The Perceptual Cycle Model and visual-search theory are **accurately described** and explicitly tied to the planned letter-search task, showing how expectations guide attention.  Although the theoretical exposition is not extraordinarily detailed, the essential mechanisms (schema vs. environmental input) and the predicted effect on search time are clearly linked to the investigation, satisfying the âdescribed and explained linkâ requirement.  A concise treatment justifies a 5 rather than the full 6.

Findings are **discussed** in relation to both Neisserâs original results and the Perceptual Cycle Model, including theoretical reasoning (feature detection, context).  The discussion is coherent but not exhaustive, justifying a score of 5 within the highest band.

The student states a precise aim â determining whether the target letter âZâ is detected faster in round or angular lists â and then **explains why this matters**, drawing on the Perceptual Cycle Model and practical examples (e.g., tumour detection, hunting).  The relevance is more than merely identified; a clear rationale connecting cognitive theory to real-world significance is offered, fulfilling the descriptor for the top band.  Minor brevity in the real-world link keeps the work at the lower end of that band (5 rather than 6).

Multiple strengths and limitations (design, sampling, materials) are identified and **explained** in direct connection to the investigationâs validity and reliability.  The treatment is thoughtful though occasionally repetitive, meriting a solid but not perfect score in the top band.

Key controlled variables (quiet room, identical instructions, identical lists, exclusion of psychology students) are listed **with brief justifications**.  The explanation is present but somewhat cursory and omits discussion of control over timing equipment; therefore the work fits the lower half of the explanatory band.

Each limitation discussed is followed by a **specific, justified modification** (spacing trials over days, testing at different times of day, including older adults).  The links are explicit and rational, fulfilling the rubric for comprehensive justification, though limited depth keeps the mark at 5 rather than 6.

The student not only describes but **explains** the choice of a repeated-measures design, contrasting it with an independent design and linking the choice to control of individual differences.  The rationale demonstrates understanding of methodological consequences, meeting the explanation criterion (upper band) at a solid level.

Appropriate descriptive statistics and a paired-samples t-test are applied; degrees of freedom, t-value, p-value and confidence interval are all reported consistently with the raw data, indicating **accurate application**.  Minor formatting issues (comma instead of decimal) are trivial, so the analysis meets high-band standards, warranting 5.

annotations.comment: The introduction provides a clear explanation of the Perceptual Cycle Model, but citations from nonâpeer-reviewed sources such as SKYbrary lack academic rigor. Consider replacing or supplementing with primary literature to strengthen theoretical grounding.
Key term âvisual searchâ is referenced without a precise operational definition. Define it explicitly in the introduction to ensure clarity for readers unfamiliar with the concept.
The student accurately connects Neisserâs original visual search study to their own letter-search task, demonstrating a strong link to background theory.
The rationale connecting PCM to real-world tasks (e.g., tumor detection) is well articulated, reinforcing the investigationâs relevance.
The null and research hypotheses state IV and DV correctly, but they could be more precise by explicitly operationalizing the timing method (e.g., start/stop procedure).
The repeated-measures design is explained, but the term âinvalidâ is misused. Replace with âuncontrolled individual differencesâ to accurately describe the designâs advantage.
Convenience sampling is described, but the rationale for representativeness is limited. Discuss how demographic composition may affect external validity.
Controlled variables are listed clearly, but no mention is made of stopwatch calibration or inter-rater timing reliability. Address timing equipment precision to strengthen control.
Table 1 is well organized and clearly presents descriptive statistics, facilitating comparison between conditions.
The interpretation omits effect size despite mentioning a percentage difference. Compute and report Cohenâs d to quantify practical significance.
Ensure units of measure (seconds) are consistently included when reporting means and ranges in the narrative.
Graph 1 lacks error bars and clear axis units, which impairs interpretation of variability. Add these to strengthen graphical presentation.
The t-test description cites p < 0.0008 but omits t (25) = 3.83. Include the test statistic and degrees of freedom for completeness.
The finding that angular distractors slow detection is clearly interpreted and linked to the hypothesis, demonstrating strong analysis.
The discussion links results back to Neisser (1964) and the PCM, effectively situating findings within background theory.
Discussion could be enriched by considering alternative cognitive theories (e.g., feature integration theory) to deepen evaluation.
Strengths/limitations section acknowledges testing effect but omits sample gender imbalance. Discuss potential bias from 77% female participants.
The suggestion to test on different days addresses order effects, but also propose using counterbalanced trial sequences to mitigate carryover.
The conclusion reiterates p < 0.0008 but should also summarize effect size and practical implications for real-world tasks.
The concluding sentence omits mention of the t-statistic and confidence interval. Include these to reinforce statistical conclusions.
References show inconsistent APA formatting (missing DOIs, improper capitalization). Review APA guidelines and standardize each entry.
Entries are not alphabetized (e.g., Neisser appears after APA Dictionary). Reorder references alphabetically by author surname.
GraphPad reference lacks author or year. Replace with full APA citation (author, year) rather than generic site name.
At the start of the Analysis section, include a brief roadmap describing the planned descriptive and inferential tests to improve reader orientation.
